steps.title: Use the first-language limit
steps.content: The underlined claim says many people in heavily Swahili-speaking countries speak Swahili as a second, third, or fourth language rather than as a first language. The passage gives a key benchmark: only up to 15 million people worldwide are estimated to speak Swahili as a first language. Data supporting the claim should show a country with far more Swahili speakers than that worldwide first-language total.

steps.title: Compare Tanzania to the benchmark
steps.content: Tanzania has about 61 million Swahili speakers, which is much larger than the estimated worldwide total of first-language Swahili speakers. Since one country alone has many more Swahili speakers than the global first-language estimate, many of those speakers must be using Swahili as a later-learned language. Choice B misstates the Tanzanian speaker count, Choice C invents a location for all first-language speakers, and Choice D compares countries without addressing first-language status. The answer is A.
